Document:
# Voice Procedure
Voice procedure is a standard way to pass information over a shared radio net.
Using consistent callsigns and prowords makes transmissions shorter, clearer, and easier to acknowledge.
## Basic Rules
- [bold]Listen before transmitting.[/bold] Do not interrupt traffic already in progress unless the message is urgent. - [bold]Plan the message first.[/bold] Know what you need to say before opening the channel. - [bold]Use callsigns.[/bold] Do not identify personnel by character name or rank over the radio. - [bold]Keep transmissions brief.[/bold] Split long messages into clear sections. - [bold]Acknowledge orders and requests.[/bold] Confirm that important traffic was received and understood. - [bold]Use plain language.[/bold] Use prowords where they apply, but avoid unnecessary jargon.
## Basic Call Format
Address the receiving station first, then identify the transmitting station.
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC 6, this is ALPHA ROMEO, message, over.[/color]
A complete call normally contains:
- The callsign being addressed. - [bold]THIS IS[/bold]. - The transmitting callsign. - The message. - [bold]OVER[/bold] or [bold]OUT[/bold].
The receiving station answers using the same order:
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ROMEO, this is HAVOC 6, send it, over.[/color]
After both stations have established contact, [bold]THIS IS[/bold] may be omitted:
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ROMEO, HAVOC 6, send it, over.[/color]
## Prowords
Prowords are standardized words with specific meanings.
[bold]Proword[/bold]
[bold]Meaning[/bold]
THIS IS
The callsign that follows identifies the transmitting station.
OVER
The transmission is complete and a reply is expected.
OUT
The exchange is complete and no reply is expected. Do not use “over and out.”
ROGER
The previous transmission was received and understood. It does not mean the instruction will be carried out.
WILCO
The instruction was understood and will be carried out. WILCO already includes the meaning of ROGER.
SAY AGAIN
Repeat the previous transmission, or a specified part of it.
CORRECTION
An error was made. The corrected information follows.
DISREGARD
Ignore the previous transmission.
BREAK
Separates sections of a message or traffic addressed to different stations.
WAIT
Pause briefly. The transmitting station will continue shortly.
WAIT OUT
A longer pause is required. The transmitting station will call again later.
HOW COPY
Confirm whether the preceding message was received clearly.
RADIO CHECK
Report signal strength and readability.
LIMA CHARLIE
Loud and clear.
ACTUAL
Identifies the person assigned to a command station rather than someone transmitting on their behalf.
INTERROGATIVE
The following traffic is a question.
BE ADVISED
The following traffic is information that may affect the receiving station.
ALL STATIONS
The message is addressed to every station on the net.
Use [bold]SAY AGAIN[/bold] instead of “repeat” when requesting a repeated radio message. On a fire-support net, [bold]REPEAT[/bold] may be interpreted as an instruction to fire the previous mission again.
## Radio Check
A radio check confirms that two stations can hear each other.
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC ROMEO, this is ALPHA ROMEO, radio check, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ROMEO, this is HAVOC ROMEO, Lima Charlie. How me? Over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]Lima Charlie, out.[/color]
When the signal is not clear, describe the problem instead:
- Weak but readable. - Broken. - Distorted. - Unreadable.
## Contact Report
A contact report should state:
- What was seen. - Where it was seen. - Approximate strength. - What the reporting element is doing.
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC 6, this is ALPHA ACTUAL, contact. Infantry, squad strength, east treeline near grid two-four-five. Engaging, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ACTUAL, HAVOC 6, roger. BRAVO is moving to your flank. Hold your position, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC 6, ALPHA ACTUAL, wilco, out.[/color]
## Fire-Support Request
Establish contact before sending the full request.
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC OPS, this is ALPHA ROMEO, fire mission, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ROMEO, HAVOC OPS, send it, over.[/color]
The request should identify:
- The target. - The target location. - The position of friendly forces. - The requested effect.
[color=#a4885c]Enemy machine-gun position on the north ridge beside the communications tower. Friendlies are two-five-zero meters east, marked by smoke. Request suppression, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ROMEO, HAVOC OPS, roger. Rounds out, impact in three-zero seconds, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC OPS, ALPHA ROMEO, impact observed. Target suppressed. End of mission, out.[/color]
## Casualty Evacuation Request
Establish contact before sending casualty details.
[color=#a4885c]HAVOC ROMEO, this is BRAVO ROMEO, casualty evacuation request, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]BRAVO ROMEO, HAVOC ROMEO, send it, over.[/color]
Include:
- Number of casualties. - Medical priority. - Pickup location. - Condition of the pickup area. - Identification or marking.
[color=#a4885c]Two casualties. One urgent surgical, one walking wounded. Pickup at the landing zone south of the bridge. Area is secure and marked with green smoke, over.[/color]
[color=#a4885c]BRAVO ROMEO, HAVOC ROMEO, good copy. Evacuation craft inbound in five minutes, out.[/color]
## Using ACTUAL
[bold]ACTUAL[/bold] identifies the person in charge speaking personally rather than an RTO or other person speaking on their behalf.
Squad leaders carry it as their assigned station: [bold]ALPHA ACTUAL[/bold] is the Alpha squad leader. Command stations append it when the officer takes the handset personally.
[color=#a4885c]ALPHA ACTUAL, this is HAVOC 6 ACTUAL. Move past phase line DODGE, over.[/color]
Without [bold]ACTUAL[/bold], [bold]HAVOC 6[/bold] traffic may be the RTO relaying for the commander. With it, the commander is speaking personally.
## Corrections
Use [bold]CORRECTION[/bold] when an error is noticed during the transmission.
[color=#a4885c]Enemy infantry west of the bridge, CORRECTION, east of the bridge, squad strength, over.[/color]
Use [bold]DISREGARD[/bold] when the entire previous message should be ignored.
[color=#a4885c]DISREGARD my last, out.[/color]
## Common Mistakes
[bold]Avoid[/bold]
[bold]Reason[/bold]
Using names or ranks
Radio traffic should identify stations by callsign.
Calling “command” without a callsign
The intended receiving station may be unclear.
Long unbroken transmissions
Other stations cannot acknowledge or pass urgent traffic.
“Over and out”
OVER requests a reply, while OUT ends the exchange.
“Roger wilco”
WILCO already confirms receipt and compliance.
“Can you repeat that?”
Use SAY AGAIN to avoid confusion on fire-support nets.
Vague reports
State the event, location, strength, and required action clearly.
Brief, structured transmissions leave the net available for acknowledgements and urgent traffic.
